draft.introduction: The Instruction of Amenemope (c. 1100 BCE) is an Egyptian wisdom text with striking parallels to the biblical Book of Proverbs 22:17–24:22. The question of direct literary borrowing has been debated since the text’s publication in 1923.

translation.text: Give your ears, hear the sayings, give your heart to understand them. It is profitable to put them in your heart, but woe to him who neglects them. Let them rest in the casket of your belly, that they may be a peg in your tongue.
translation.original_text: dj msdr.wj=k, sḏm md.wt, dj jb=k r sḫā st. nfr pw rd.t st m ḥā.t(j)=k

translator_notes.note: Compare Proverbs 22:17–18: “Incline your ear and hear the words of the wise, and apply your heart to my knowledge.” The parallels are so close that most scholars now accept some form of literary dependence, though the direction and mechanism remain debated.
